message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Successful public health regimes for COVID-19 push below unity long-term global
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  –the average number of secondary cases caused by an infectious individual. Most assessments use
                  <jats:italic>local</jats:italic>
                  information. Populations differ in
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  , amongst themselves and over time. We use a SIR model for two populations to make the conceptual point that even if each locality averages
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  < 1, the overall epidemic can still grow, provided these populations have asynchronous variation in transmission, and are coupled by movement of infectious individuals. This emergent effect in pandemic dynamics instantiates “Parrondo’s Paradox,” -- an entity comprised of distinct but interacting units can behave qualitatively differently than each part on its own. For effective COVID-19 disease mitigation strategies, it is critical that infectious individuals moving among locations be identified and quarantined. This does not warrant indiscriminate prevention of movement, but rather rational, targeted testing and national coordination.
                </jats:p>
title: Regional COVID-19 spread despite expected declines: how mitigation is hindered by spatio-temporal variation in local control measures

reference.article-title: Paradoxical persistence through mixed-system dynamics: towards a unified perspective of reversal behaviours in evolutionary ecology
Populations can persist in an environment consisting of sink habitats only
Paradoxical Survival: Examining the Parrondo Effect across Biology

reference.unstructured: C. Rivers , E. Martin , D. Meyer , T. Inglesby , A. Cicero (2020) Modernizing and Expanding Outbreak Science to Support Better Decision Making During Public Health Crises: Lessons for COVID-19 and Beyond. in Report, March (Johns Hopkins University, Johns Hopkins Center for Health Security), p 26.
